_meta.what: Wave 1 census — one record per tradition family (mythology-research-spec §2.1). Assembled 2026-07-18 from the 26-agent census fan-out; candidate lists in census_candidates.jsonl.

traditions.name: Abrahamic Folk Corpus (angels, demons, saints, golem)
traditions.region: Near East, Mediterranean, Europe, Americas (global diaspora)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: The sacred core (God, prophets, founders) is HARD-excluded per binding rules. Islamic named sacred figures are never depicted; jinn as a folk class are censusable but belief in jinn is live doctrine for ~2B Muslims, so all jinn-class entries carry 'living'. Archangels (Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, Azrael) are actively venerated within Christianity and named in Islamic tradition — depictable only under the messenger carve-out (corporate staff of investor institutions, suits not halos), flagged 'living' and owner-gated. Folk saints: Santa Muerte has 10-12M living devotees (Chesnut estimate, verified) and is condemned by the Catholic Church — doubly sensitive; Maximon is held by active Tz'utujil cofradias whose ritual specifics are community-held, hence 'restricted'. Demonological material (Goetia, Lilith, Lucifer) has no mainstream worship but fringe theistic-Satanist and neopagan Lilith veneration exists; main risk is offense to living Christians/Jews/Muslims, not to worshippers of the demons. Antisemitic-trope material (e.g. Wandering Jew) is excluded outright, see closed notes. Web verification succeeded through the proxy; claims checked against Wikipedia-indexed academic sources one level down.
sources: Public-domain primary texts: Hebrew Bible / KJV & JPS translations; Babylonian Talmud (Soncino); 1 Enoch (R.H. Charles trans., 1917); Testament of Solomon (Conybeare trans., 1898); Lesser Key of Solomon / Goetia (Mathers-Crowley, 1904); Alphabet of Ben Sira (Lilith legend)
R. Andrew Chesnut, Devoted to Death: Santa Muerte, the Skeleton Saint (Oxford UP) — devotee estimates verified via patheos.com interview and en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santa_Muerte
Gershom Scholem, Kabbalah and On the Kabbalah and Its Symbolism (golem, Metatron, Samael)
en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gad_(deity) + biblehub.com/commentaries/isaiah/65-11.htm (Gad/Meni luck deities, verified)
en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maximón + nationalgeographic.com (cofradía custodianship, verified)
Encyclopaedia Judaica / Jewish Encyclopedia (1906, PD) for angelology and demonology entries

traditions.name: Arthurian & Medieval European Legendary Cycles
traditions.region: Britain, Wales, Brittany, France, Western Europe
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Almost entirely literary legend with no cultic history — influence floor is 1 for nearly every entity (Väinämöinen calibration: national-literary fame without worship). ONE narrow exception: the Glastonbury Goddess Temple (registered place of worship since 2003) venerates the Lady of Avalon and the Nine Morgens, explicitly identifying Morgen la Fey among them — a small organized revival, so Morgan le Fay carries flag 'living' and sets the influence ceiling at 3 (revival tier); no other entity here can exceed 1. The Holy Grail is a legendary literary object but is entangled with living Christian eucharistic symbolism — recommend owner review before irreverent casino use, though it is not itself an object of liturgical worship. All source texts are public domain (Malory, Geoffrey of Monmouth, Chrétien, Mabinogion, Gawain-poet, Child ballads); flag ip:public_domain_literary applies list-wide, and later waves must avoid distinctive elements original to in-copyright retellings (T.H. White, Marion Zimmer Bradley, BBC Merlin, Fate franchise designs). Web verification was limited to the living-worship question; the rest rests on well-attested PD primary texts plus search confirmation.
sources: Thomas Malory, Le Morte d'Arthur (1485; PD, Caxton/Winchester texts)
Geoffrey of Monmouth, Historia Regum Britanniae + Vita Merlini (12th c.; PD translations)
Chrétien de Troyes, Arthurian romances incl. Perceval and Lancelot (12th c.; PD translations)
The Mabinogion, esp. Culhwch and Olwen (Charlotte Guest / Ford translations, PD)
Sir Gawain and the Green Knight (14th c.; PD editions/translations)
Lancelot-Grail (Vulgate) Cycle and Post-Vulgate (13th c.; PD-era translations)
Wolfram von Eschenbach, Parzival (13th c.; PD translations — Lohengrin source)
F.J. Child, English and Scottish Popular Ballads vol. III (Robin Hood ballads, Tam Lin; PD)
Walter Map, De Nugis Curialium (King Herla; PD translation)
Jean d'Arras, Roman de Mélusine (14th c.)
Glastonbury Goddess Temple — goddesstemple.co.uk (living-revival verification for Lady of Avalon / Nine Morgens)

traditions.name: Buddhist cosmology
traditions.region: South, Southeast, East, and Central Asia (pan-Asian; global diaspora)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Living major religion (>300M adherents): nearly every deity-class candidate carries the living flag and is owner-gated for on-screen casino roles — default posture is off-screen investor. The historical Buddha is categorically excluded (founder of a living major). Vajrayana adds a second gate: anuttarayoga tantra deities (Kalachakra, Cakrasamvara, Hevajra, Guhyasamaja) are empowerment-restricted practices — reported in closed_material_notes, not collected; Yamantaka and dakinis are listed only via their widely published public iconography with flag restricted. Dorje Shugden is excluded entirely (live sectarian controversy). Web tools worked through the proxy; claims verified via Wikipedia-as-index plus Britannica and public-domain canon translations. Source quality is 5 — the Pali Canon, Lotus Sutra, and jataka corpus have rich public-domain translations (Access to Insight, SuttaCentral, 84000, sacred-texts).
sources: Access to Insight / SuttaCentral (public-domain and open Pali Canon translations)
84000.co (open-access Kangyur translations)
Encyclopaedia Britannica — dharmapala and Buddhist deity entries
Buswell & Lopez, The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism (academic reference)
Wikipedia (index only, claims verified one level down: Vaisravana, Jambhala, Twenty-Four Protective Deities)

traditions.name: Celtic mythology (Irish, Welsh, Gaulish)
traditions.region: Ireland, Wales, Gaul (France/Britain); diaspora worldwide
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Uneven by sub-tradition: Irish (source_quality 5 — rich PD primary translations: Táin, Cath Maige Tuired, Lebor Gabála) and Welsh (4 — Four Branches of the Mabinogi, PD Guest translation) are deep; Gaulish is 1-2 — no native narratives survive, only epigraphy, iconography, and hostile Roman notices (Lucan, Caesar) through interpretatio romana; the 4 is an honest weighted average and Gaulish figures should not receive deep myth profiles later. Organized revival worship is real (Neo-Druidry, Celtic Reconstructionist polytheism, active devotion to the Morrígan, Brigid, Lugh, Cernunnos) — 'living'-flagged figures should be owner-gated for on-screen roles per depiction_risk=low/living policy. Brigid is syncretized with the living Christian cult of St. Brigid of Kildare (major Irish saint, feast Feb 1 now an Irish public holiday) — handle the goddess/saint boundary carefully. Nothing in this tradition is culturally closed; all material is fully published and public-sphere. Scope note: Scottish Gaelic, Manx, Breton, and Cornish sub-traditions (kelpie, selkie, Ankou, etc.) were NOT scoped into this family assignment and are absent, not judged uninteresting; Arthurian matter is treated as a separate tradition. Recognition ceiling 5 is carried by the leprechaun (global household image); patron-god ceiling is 4 (Morrígan, Cernunnos, Cú Chulainn).
sources: Táin Bó Cúailnge (PD translations: Dunn 1914; Faraday 1904)
Cath Maige Tuired / Second Battle of Mag Tuired (Stokes 1891 PD; Gray 1982)
Lebor Gabála Érenn (Macalister translation, Irish Texts Society)
The Mabinogion (Charlotte Guest PD translation; Sioned Davies 2007)
Lucan, Pharsalia I.444-446 (Taranis/Esus/Teutates passage) and Caesar, De Bello Gallico VI.17-18
James MacKillop, A Dictionary of Celtic Mythology (Oxford)
Miranda Green, Dictionary of Celtic Myth and Legend (Thames & Hudson)

Wikipedia as index (Cernunnos, Taranis, Gallo-Roman religion — claims verified one level down): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cernunnos ; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taranis ; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gallo-Roman_religion
Britannica, Celtic religion: https://www.britannica.com/topic/Celtic-religion
Revival-worship verification (web search, 2026): Celtic Reconstructionism and OBOD Druidry overviews

traditions.name: Chinese folk religion & Taoist pantheon
traditions.region: China, Taiwan, Hong Kong, and the Chinese diaspora (Southeast Asia and worldwide)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: This is a LIVING MAJOR tradition: several hundred million people practice Chinese folk religion/Taoism today (Caishen and Jade Emperor veneration alone spans well over 100M at Lunar New Year), so nearly every top god carries the 'living' flag and is owner-gated for on-screen roles. Ruling D-4 removes several of the tradition's most famous figures (Guan Yu, Mazu — both deified historical humans; see closed_material_notes), which noticeably lowers the roster below what pop-culture familiarity would suggest. Additional care: PRC audiences are sensitive to disrespectful depictions of venerated figures; Nezha and Sun Wukong are current blockbuster properties in China (the figures themselves are public domain, the recent films are not). Source picture: superb public-domain literary primaries (Journey to the West, Fengshen Yanyi, Shanhaijing, Werner 1922) but the folk-cult practice side rests on academic secondary work, hence 4 not 5. Web verification was performed via search results plus my own verified knowledge; fetches through the proxy were limited to search summaries.
sources: Journey to the West (Wu Cheng'en; public-domain translations incl. Timothy Richard 1913; Anthony Yu for reference)
Fengshen Yanyi / Investiture of the Gods (Ming novel, public domain)
Shanhaijing / Classic of Mountains and Seas (trans. Anne Birrell as academic reference)
E.T.C. Werner, Myths and Legends of China (1922, public domain)
Wikipedia articles on Chinese folk religion, Jade Emperor, Eight Immortals (used as index; claims verified one level down)
World History Encyclopedia — Jade Emperor; historyskills.com — Jade Emperor worship today
Academic estimates of Chinese folk-religion adherence (Pew Research Center religious-landscape data)

traditions.name: Ancient Egyptian mythology
traditions.region: North Africa — Nile Valley (Egypt/Sudan); Greco-Roman diffusion across the Mediterranean
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Ancient cult is extinct, but a small open-practice Kemetic revival exists (broader Kemetism from the 1970s; Kemetic Orthodoxy / House of Netjer founded 1988 by Tamara Siuda — membership in the low thousands at most, no published 2025 figures). Revival is reconstructionist and publishes openly; nothing is ritually closed and there are no depiction restrictions, so depiction_risk for flagged deities is low (revival), not full living-gate. All god-class candidates carry the 'living' flag because revivalists venerate the netjeru broadly — owner review recommended but risk is low. Minor commercial note: 'Isis' has an unfortunate modern acronym collision to watch in marketing copy. Web verification worked through the proxy; claims checked against Wikipedia-indexed and Egyptology reference sources.
sources: R.O. Faulkner, The Ancient Egyptian Pyramid Texts / The Ancient Egyptian Book of the Dead (primary translations)
E.A. Wallis Budge, The Book of the Dead (public domain translation)
Miriam Lichtheim, Ancient Egyptian Literature vols. I–III (Tale of Two Brothers, Setne cycle, Westcar Papyrus)
Richard H. Wilkinson, The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt (Thames & Hudson, 2003)
Geraldine Pinch, Egyptian Mythology: A Guide (Oxford, 2002)
Wikipedia as index (Kemetism, Kemetic Orthodoxy, individual deity articles) — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kemetism
Ancient Egypt Online (ancientegyptonline.co.uk) — deity reference incl. Shai
Tour Egypt / Global Egyptian Museum glossary (Shai, fate deities)

traditions.name: Greek and Roman mythology
traditions.region: Greece, Aegean, Italy, wider Mediterranean (Hellenistic and Roman world)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: No culturally closed material — the corpus is fully public-sphere with rich public-domain primaries. A modern organized revival exists (Hellenism / Hellenic polytheism; YSEE legally recognized as a 'known religion' in Greece in April 2017), so worship is not strictly extinct; however, per the frozen Wave 0 calibration (Zeus = influence 2, depiction_risk none) revival scale is treated as below the influence-3 bar and candidates are NOT flagged 'living' — if the owner later upgrades revival weight, the whole Olympian roster moves together. Hecate additionally has substantial modern Wiccan/neopagan devotion. Web verification was done via search results (Wikipedia-as-index plus revival-news coverage); claims here also rest on the standard PD primary corpus.
sources: Hesiod, Theogony & Works and Days (public-domain translations, e.g. Evelyn-White, Perseus Digital Library)
Homer, Iliad & Odyssey (PD translations, Perseus Digital Library)
Ovid, Metamorphoses & Fasti (PD translations)
Apollodorus, Bibliotheca (PD translation, Frazer)
Pausanias, Description of Greece (PD translation)
Theoi Project (theoi.com) — sourced classical-text compendium
Wikipedia (index only; claims verified one level down)
The Wild Hunt / YSEE coverage of 2017 Greek legal recognition of Hellenic polytheism (wildhunt.org)

traditions.name: Hindu
traditions.region: South Asia (India, Nepal, Bali) + global diaspora
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Living major religion (~1.2B adherents, third largest worldwide) — essentially every deity listed carries depiction_risk 'living' and is flagged so; per game frame they default to off-screen investors, on-screen roles owner-gated. Real-world precedent of strong community objection to disrespectful commercial depictions of deities (esp. Shiva, Rama, Sita, Lakshmi on products/games) — treat reverently. No formal closure on the public mythological corpus (epics and Puranas are widely published and translated), but esoteric tantric material (initiation rites, specific bija mantras) is guru-transmitted and was not collected. Web fetches worked; claims cross-checked against public-domain translations (Ganguli Mahabharata, Griffith Rig Veda/Ramayana) and encyclopedia-level sources. Uniquely rich native gambling mythology: Shiva-Parvati dice game (Skanda/Padma Purana) with the Diwali gambling decree, the Mahabharata dice-game catastrophe, Nala possessed by the dice-demon Kali, and the Rig Veda 'Gambler's Hymn' (RV 10.34).
sources: The Mahabharata, K.M. Ganguli translation (public domain, sacred-texts.com)
Rig Veda, R.T.H. Griffith translation incl. the Gambler's Hymn RV 10.34 (public domain)
The Ramayana, R.T.H. Griffith translation (public domain)
Skanda Purana / Padma Purana accounts of the Shiva-Parvati dice game (via talkingmyths.com and enrouteindianhistory.com, cross-checked)
Pew Research Center / Wikipedia 'Hindus' and 'Hinduism by country' for adherent figures (index-level, verified one level down)
Wikipedia as index for individual deity/epic articles (claims verified against primary translations where load-bearing)

traditions.name: Japanese mythology & Shinto kami
traditions.region: Japan (East Asia)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Shinto is a living majority practice (80,000+ shrines; ~48-80% of Japan participates in shrine practice even though few self-identify as 'Shintoist'), so every venerated kami carries the living flag and is owner-gated for on-screen roles. Specific hot spots: (1) Yasukuni Shrine and its enshrined war dead are politically explosive — avoid entirely. (2) The physical Imperial Regalia are never publicly displayed and are tied to the living imperial institution; their MYTHIC versions (Kojiki/Nihongi) are fully public and listed as artifacts. (3) Many named kami are deified real humans and are excluded per D-4 (see closed_material_notes); Hachiman is retained because the identification with Emperor Ojin attaches to a figure of legendary, not established, historicity — owner may still gate. (4) Ainu and Ryukyuan religions are SEPARATE living traditions, not sub-sets of Shinto — not covered here and should get their own tradition records if wanted. (5) Yokai/folk beings have no worship and are risk-none per family guidance. Web verification succeeded through the proxy; scores rest on searched sources plus verified background knowledge.
sources: Kojiki, trans. Basil Hall Chamberlain (1882) — public domain, Wikisource / Internet Archive
Nihongi (Nihon Shoki), trans. W.G. Aston (1896) — public domain, Wikisource
Encyclopedia of Shinto, Kokugakuin University (online academic reference)
Religion in Japan — Wikipedia (used as index; adherence figures cross-checked)
Statista, Number of Shintoists in Japan 2010-2023 / religious affiliation 2021
Nippon.com, 'Religious Adherents Outnumber People in Japan' (Agency for Cultural Affairs statistics)

traditions.name: Mesoamerican mythology (Aztec / Mexica and Maya)
traditions.region: Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, western Honduras/El Salvador (Mesoamerica)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Maya religion is a LIVING minority practice: millions of highland and Yucatec Maya maintain continuous (often Catholic-syncretized) worship — ajq'ij daykeepers still pray to Popol Vuh creator forces, Ch'a Cháak rain ceremonies still petition Chaac. Figures flagged 'living' should default to off-screen/owner-gated roles. Aztec-side worship is extinct with a small Mexicayotl revival (low depiction risk). Additional cautions: (1) daykeeper calendar-divination knowledge is initiatory — indexed as closed, not collected; (2) Maximón/San Simón is an active, cofradía-controlled cult — listed for owner review only; (3) do NOT treat Santa Muerte as Aztec continuity (contested modern movement, excluded); (4) human-sacrifice imagery needs tasteful handling; sources on Aztec religion are filtered through colonial chroniclers. Web verification of contemporary practice done via search; entity claims verified against standard academic references and my own knowledge of the primary texts.
sources: Popol Vuh (Ximénez manuscript; Christenson and Tedlock translations; Brasseur de Bourbourg 1861 and Spence 1908 editions public domain)
Florentine Codex (Bernardino de Sahagún, 16th c.; General History of the Things of New Spain)
Books of Chilam Balam (Yucatec Maya, colonial-era)
Dresden Codex and Codex Borgia (pre-Columbian screenfolds, public domain facsimiles)
Miller & Taube, An Illustrated Dictionary of the Gods and Symbols of Ancient Mexico and the Maya (Thames & Hudson)
Smithsonian NMAI 'Living Maya Time' — contemporary calendar/daykeeper practice (https://maya.nmai.si.edu/corn-and-maya-time/corn-and-calendar-traditions)
Slate: 'Do people still practice the Mayan religion?' (https://slate.com/human-interest/2012/12/end-of-world-2012-do-people-still-practice-the-mayan-religion.html)
Wikipedia (index only; claims verified one level down): Maya religion, Popol Vuh (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maya_religion, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Popol_Vuh)

traditions.name: Mesopotamian mythology (Sumerian, Akkadian, Babylonian, Assyrian)
traditions.region: Iraq / Syria (ancient Sumer, Akkad, Babylonia, Assyria)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: A small reconstructionist revival exists (Temple of Sumer online community; 'Kaldanism' as an umbrella for Babylonian reconstruction) but it is marginal — far smaller than Hellenic revival, and Zeus is calibrated at influence 2, so the ceiling here is 2, not 3; the four main revival targets (Inanna/Ishtar, Marduk, Enki, Ashur) carry the 'living' flag conservatively for owner review, depiction risk low. Separate ETHNIC sensitivity, not worship: Ashur and lamassu imagery are heritage symbols for modern Assyrian (Christian) communities, and much physical heritage was destroyed by ISIS 2014-2017 — depict respectfully, no closure applies. All primary texts are fully published and public-scholarship material; nothing is tradition-restricted. No dedicated luck/gambling deity exists — fate is administered bureaucratically (the Tablet of Destinies, the namtar/shimtu fate concept, and the gods casting lots to divide the cosmos in Atrahasis); Namtar and the Tablet carry the luck_gambling flag as the closest fate-office holders.
sources: ORACC — Ancient Mesopotamian Gods and Goddesses (AMGG), Univ. of Pennsylvania (oracc.museum.upenn.edu/amgg) — scholarly entries on the ~50 most important deities
ETCSL — Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature, Oxford (freely available translations: Inanna's Descent, Enki and the World Order, Lugalbanda, Gilgamesh Sumerian poems)
L. W. King, 'The Seven Tablets of Creation' (Enuma Elish), 1902 — public domain
R. Campbell Thompson, 'The Epic of Gilgamish', 1928 — public domain
Jeremy Black & Anthony Green, 'Gods, Demons and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia' (academic reference)
Stephanie Dalley, 'Myths from Mesopotamia' (Oxford World's Classics — academic secondary, not PD)
Temple of Sumer (templeofsumer.org) + Semitic-neopaganism survey material — revival-worship status check only

traditions.name: Norse and Continental Germanic Mythology
traditions.region: Scandinavia, Iceland, and continental Germanic Europe (incl. Anglo-Saxon England)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Organized revival worship exists: Ásatrú/Heathenry (Ásatrúarfélagið in Iceland, ~5,500 members by 2022, state-recognized since 1973; broader worldwide heathen communities) — major pantheon gods carry flag 'living', depiction_risk low (revival, not restricted). MAJOR co-opted-symbol sensitivity: runes (othala, sowilo), Mjölnir pendants, valknut, 'sonnenrad'/black sun, and Odinist framing have been appropriated by white-supremacist and neo-völkisch groups; mainstream inclusive heathenry (Declaration 127 signatories) explicitly rejects this. Art direction should avoid the specific co-opted symbol renderings and any blood-and-soil framing. Continental variants (Wotan/Woden = Odin, Donar/Thunor = Thor, Frija = Frigg) are treated as the same identities, not separate entities. Marvel's Thor/Loki/Hela/Heimdall designs are protected IP — mythological figures themselves are public domain; flagged ip:modern_ip_flagged where the association is strong. Web search worked through the proxy; claims verified against search results plus public-domain primary-text knowledge.
sources: Poetic Edda (Bellows translation, 1923, public domain)
Prose Edda / Snorra Edda (Brodeur translation, 1916, public domain)
Völsunga saga and Nibelungenlied (public domain translations)
Beowulf (public domain translations)
Saxo Grammaticus, Gesta Danorum (Elton translation, public domain)
Tacitus, Germania (public domain)
Rudolf Simek, Dictionary of Northern Mythology (academic reference)
John Lindow, Norse Mythology: A Guide (academic reference)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%81satr%C3%BAarf%C3%A9lagi%C3%B0 (revival-worship status, used as index and verified)
https://asatru.is/ (Ásatrúarfélagið official site)

traditions.name: Polynesian and Māori mythology
traditions.region: Polynesian triangle — Aotearoa New Zealand, Hawaii, Tahiti/Society Islands, Samoa, Tonga, Cook Islands, Rapa Nui
traditions.sensitivity_notes: These are LIVING indigenous cultures, not extinct pantheons. Pele receives active, documented present-day veneration (offerings at Kīlauea, chants, hoʻokupu during the 2018 and later eruptions) — on-screen use is owner-gated 'living'. Māori atua are invoked in living karakia and mātauranga Māori; there is a documented public backlash record against games using Māori material without consultation (H1Z1 'Maui' caricature; The Spinoff / Te Ao Māori News coverage 2019–2025), so all Māori-specific figures should be treated as consultation-recommended even where flagged only 'living' or unflagged. Whakapapa (genealogy) is taonga: figures who are iwi ancestors are excluded or flagged restricted. Source picture: rich public-domain 19th–early-20th-c collections (Grey 1855, Tregear 1891, Westervelt 1916, Fornander, Malo) plus strong academic secondaries (Beckwith 1940, Te Ara) = 4; the deepest esoteric layer (whare wānanga lore) is deliberately not in the public record and caps extraction below 5. Web verification succeeded through the proxy; searches used for the living-worship and sensitivity claims.
sources: George Grey, Polynesian Mythology and Ancient Traditional History of the New Zealand Race (1855, public domain)
Edward Tregear, The Maori-Polynesian Comparative Dictionary (1891, public domain)
W. D. Westervelt, Hawaiian Legends of Volcanoes (1916) and Legends of Ma-ui (1910, public domain)
David Malo, Hawaiian Antiquities (Moolelo Hawaii, trans. Emerson 1903, public domain)
Martha Beckwith, Hawaiian Mythology (1940, academic standard reference)
Te Ara — The Encyclopedia of New Zealand (govt-published Māori mythology entries)
H. Arlo Nimmo, 'The Cult of Pele in Traditional Hawai'i', Bishop Museum Occasional Papers (academic)
The Wild Hunt (2018) and Seattle Times reporting on present-day offerings to Pele at Kīlauea
The Spinoff / Te Ao Māori News reporting on Māori representation and appropriation in video games (2019–2025)
Wikipedia (used as index only; claims verified one level down)

traditions.name: Yoruba Orishas & West African Mythology
traditions.region: West Africa (Nigeria, Benin, Ghana, Togo, Sahel) plus the Atlantic diaspora (Cuba, Brazil, Trinidad, Haiti, USA)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: This is a LIVING family: Yoruba orisha religion in Nigeria/Benin plus Santería/Lucumí (Cuba), Candomblé (Brazil), Trinidad Orisha, and Vodun (Benin/Togo) — practitioner estimates range from several million to claims of ~100M when diaspora syncretists are counted; I scored influence ceiling 4 (living significant practice, not verifiably >100M organized) to stay consistent with the Shinto/Amaterasu calibration. Every orisha and vodun listed carries the living flag and is owner-gated for on-screen roles. Specific sensitivities: Shopona/Babalú-Ayé's Yoruba name was traditionally taboo to speak (he was addressed by euphemisms) — use the diaspora name in any player-facing text; many orishas carry Catholic-saint syncretic identities that practitioners treat seriously (flagged syncretic:catholic_saints); the Ifá divination corpus and several societies are initiation-closed (see closed_material_notes). Source quality is honestly mixed: strong academic secondary literature (Bascom, Abimbola, Thompson) and public-domain colonial-era collections (Ellis 1894), but the deep primary corpus (Ifá odu verses) is orally held and initiation-restricted, which caps extraction depth. Web search worked through the proxy; claims were verified against Wikipedia-as-index plus Britannica, EBSCO, and academic references.
sources: Wikipedia (index only, claims verified one level down): Orisha, Yoruba religion, Ifá, Babalawo, Mami Wata, West African mythology
Britannica — 'Orisha' and related entries
EBSCO Research Starters — 'Mami Wata (African myth)'
World History Encyclopedia — 'Orisha'
A. B. Ellis, The Yoruba-Speaking Peoples of the Slave Coast of West Africa (1894, public domain)
William Bascom, Ifa Divination: Communication Between Gods and Men in West Africa (academic)
Wande Abimbola, Ifá: An Exposition of Ifá Literary Corpus (academic)
US Bureau of Prisons chaplaincy manual on Orisha traditions (public-sector reference on diaspora practice)

traditions.name: Andean mythology (Inca and pre-Inca)
traditions.region: Andes: Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, northern Chile/Argentina (Inca, Aymara, Moche, Chimú, Chavín, Tiwanaku, Huarochirí)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Andean religion is a LIVING syncretic practice, not a museum tradition: Pachamama veneration, apu (mountain-spirit) offerings, ch'alla libations, despacho ceremonies, and the Ekeko/Alasitas cult (UNESCO-listed 2017) are practiced by millions across Peru/Bolivia today, fused with Catholicism ('doble fe'). All living-flagged figures should be owner-gated for on-screen roles; Pachamama especially so — she is also politically charged (Bolivian constitution, Vatican Pachamama controversy 2019). Ritual specifics (despacho contents, paqo/Q'ero initiation knowledge) are practitioner-transmitted and were NOT collected. Source quality is honest 3: no native pre-conquest narrative texts survive (khipu are not readable narrative); the corpus is Spanish colonial chronicles (Cobo, Molina, Betanzos, Garcilaso — PD but filtered through missionary/imperial lenses) plus one extraordinary Quechua-language primary, the Huarochirí Manuscript (~1608). Pre-Inca cultures (Chavín, Moche, Tiwanaku) are known almost purely from iconography — names like 'Ai Apaec' and 'Staff God' are scholarly conventions, capping later-wave extraction depth for those entities. Web search worked through the proxy; claims verified against search results plus assistant knowledge of the chronicle corpus.
sources: Huarochirí Manuscript (~1608, Quechua primary; Salomon & Urioste trans. 1991; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Huarochirí_Manuscript as index)
Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653, PD chronicle)
Cristóbal de Molina, Relación de las fábulas y ritos de los incas (c.1575, PD)
Garcilaso de la Vega, Comentarios Reales de los Incas (1609, PD)
Wikipedia as index (Inca mythology, Pachamama, Ekeko, Alasitas, Aiapæc, Staff God, Apu) — claims verified one level down via search
Museo Larco online exhibition (Moche iconography, syncretism)
The Wild Hunt reporting on contemporary Pachamama practice (2025)

traditions.name: Finnish and Baltic Mythology
traditions.region: Northeastern Europe — Finland, Karelia, Lithuania, Latvia
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Two genuinely LIVING revival communities: Romuva (Lithuania) was granted state recognition by the Seimas in December 2024 after an ECHR ruling, ~5,100 self-identified in the 2011 census; Dievturība (Latvia, founded 1925, ~663 registered members 2011); Finland has small registered native-faith groups (Karhun kansa, Taivaannaula). Core Baltic deities (Perkūnas, Dievas, Laima, Žemyna, Saulė, Gabija) and Finnish Ukko/Tapio are actively venerated by these revivals — flagged 'living'; owner-gate their on-screen roles per the depiction rubric (risk level: low/revival, not major-religion living). Caveat on the Finnish side: the Kalevala is Elias Lönnrot's 19th-century literary redaction of genuine Karelian oral runo-song, so literary shaping must be separated from attested folk religion; Kalevala-specific framings are flagged ip:public_domain_literary (Crawford 1888 and Kirby 1907 translations are PD). Caveat on the Baltic side: primary attestation is thin — much comes from hostile Christian chroniclers and the Latvian dainas; several 'goddesses' (e.g. Milda) are 19th-century romantic inventions and were excluded. Source quality is uneven: Finnish ~5 (rich PD primary), Baltic ~3 (dainas rich but under-translated; Lithuanian primary fragmentary) — 4 is the honest composite; later waves should extract more cautiously on the Baltic side. Web verification was performed via search results; principal fetches succeeded.
sources: Kalevala, comp. Elias Lönnrot — PD English translations: J.M. Crawford (1888), W.F. Kirby (1907)
Latvju dainas, comp. Krišjānis Barons (Latvian folk-song corpus, PD)
Marija Gimbutas, The Balts (1963) — academic reference for Baltic pantheon
Endre Bojtár, Foreword to the Past: A Cultural History of the Baltic People (1999)
Felix Oinas / Juha Pentikäinen scholarship on Finnish folk religion and Kalevala mythology
Wikipedia (as index; claims verified one level down): Romuva (religion), Baltic neopaganism, Laima, Dalia (mythology)
The Wild Hunt (Dec 2024): Lithuania's Seimas grants state recognition to Romuva
Britannica: Laima; Law & Religion UK: ECHR Romuva ruling (2021)

traditions.name: Global Folklore Corpus (fairy-tale types and traditional cryptids)
traditions.region: Global — European-centered fairy-tale corpus (ATU types) plus Americas/worldwide reported-legend cryptids
traditions.sensitivity_notes: No living worship anywhere in this family — influence ceiling is the honest floor (1); these are content-seed beings (enemies, events, NPCs, items), not patron-capable gods. Sensitivities are (a) Indigenous roots of some 'cryptids': Sasquatch/Bigfoot derives from Halkomelem sasq'ets and Ogopogo from the Syilx (Okanagan) water being Naitaka — the modern public-sphere cryptid versions are listed, with Ogopogo flagged 'restricted' for owner review because Syilx voices have objected to the cartoonish repackaging; (b) Br'er Rabbit sits atop African-American (and ultimately West/Central African) oral tradition filtered through the contested Joel Chandler Harris compilations and Disney's shelved Song of the South — flagged 'restricted'; (c) IP: the underlying tales are public domain, but specific modern visual designs (Disney's Cinderella/Fairy Godmother/Magic Mirror, DreamWorks' Puss in Boots, Gaiman's Sandman) are trademarked/copyrighted — flagged ip:modern_ip_flagged where relevant. Web verification succeeded through the proxy; claims cross-checked against PD primary texts and academic reference indexes, with listicle-grade cryptid sites used only as an index of notoriety, never as sole source.
sources: Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen (public-domain English translations, e.g. Margaret Hunt 1884)
Perrault, Histoires ou contes du temps passé (1697; PD translations)
Hans Christian Andersen, Fairy Tales (PD translations)
Uther, Hans-Jörg, The Types of International Folktales (ATU index, 2004) — via Harvard Library folklore research guide (guides.library.harvard.edu/folk_and_myth/indices)
D. L. Ashliman, Folktexts (sites.pitt.edu/~dash/folktexts.html) — PD tale translations organized by ATU type
Washington Irving, The Legend of Sleepy Hollow (1820, PD)
Joel Chandler Harris, Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (1880, PD — provenance contested)
Point Pleasant Register (1966) / John Keel, The Mothman Prophecies — Mothman legend documentation
Contemporary cryptid coverage used as notoriety index only (Atlas Obscura ATU article; general cryptid surveys cross-checked against encyclopedia-level sources)

traditions.name: Inuit mythology
traditions.region: Arctic — Greenland, northern Canada (Nunavut/NWT/Nunavik), Alaska, Chukotka
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Living Indigenous culture. Most Inuit today are Christian, but there is an active revival of pre-Christian spirituality (notably in Greenland, invoking Sila and the Sea Woman) plus unbroken folk continuity in hunting customs and story traditions — hence influence ceiling 3 and depiction_risk low-to-living for the great spirits (Sedna, Sila, Nanook flagged 'living'; owner-gate on-screen roles). The tradition is oral and regionally variable: 'the' Inuit pantheon is a scholarly composite; names vary by region (Sedna = Nuliajuk/Nerrivik/Arnakuagsak/Arnapkapfaaluk). Primary sources are colonial-era ethnographies (Boas 1888, Rasmussen's Fifth Thule reports — substantial and partly public domain) rather than native written texts = source_quality 3; later waves should not extract deep 'canonical' myth detail beyond what these support. Web fetches were not needed beyond search verification; claims cross-checked against Wikipedia one level down plus ethnographic summaries. Figures listed here are all widely published, including by Inuit-owned presses (e.g. Inhabit Media, Nunavut); genuinely closed material is reported in closed_material_notes, not collected. No luck/gambling deity exists in this tradition — honest zero, not an omission.
sources: Franz Boas, The Central Eskimo (1888, public domain)
Knud Rasmussen, Intellectual Culture of the Iglulik Eskimos / Fifth Thule Expedition reports (1929–31)
Wikipedia: Inuit religion / Silap Inua / Qallupilluit / Kiviuq (used as index, claims verified one level down)
Canadian Geographic, 'Shamans, Spirits, and Faith in the Inuit North'
Laugrand & Oosten, Inuit Shamanism and Christianity (McGill-Queen's, 2010)
UC Berkeley Dept. of Scandinavian, 'Greenlanders embrace pre-Christian Inuit traditions' (revival evidence)

traditions.name: Korean mythology and folk religion (Muism / musok)
traditions.region: Korean Peninsula and Jeju Island; Korean diaspora
traditions.sensitivity_notes: Muism is a genuinely LIVING minority practice: 200,000+ mudang active in South Korea, gut rituals performed today, and the Jeju Chilmeoridang Yeongdeunggut (Yeongdeung Halmang rite) is UNESCO-listed (2009) — verified via web search. Deities still invoked in gut (Yongwang, Sansin, Chilseong, Samsin, Bari, Yeomra, Jeoseung Saja, Seongju, Yeongdeung Halmang, Sonnim, Dangun/Hwanung via the Daejongist revival) carry flag 'living' and should be owner-gated for on-screen roles. No formal depiction taboo exists — the material is widely published and nationally celebrated — but muism has a history of being stigmatized ('misin'/superstition), so mocking-portrayal risk is reputational rather than doctrinal. Influence ceiling 3, not 4: practice is continuous and vibrant but diffuse folk/client-based, not an organized religion with clear millions of adherents. Source quality 3: rich academic secondary literature and the National Folk Museum's Encyclopedia of Korean Folk Culture; oral muga/bonpuli epics exist mainly in modern academic transcriptions and copyrighted translations, thin public-domain primary text (Samguk Yusa translations mostly in copyright). Web fetches worked normally; key claims verified one level below Wikipedia.
sources: Encyclopedia of Korean Folk Culture, National Folk Museum of Korea (folkency.nfm.go.kr)
UNESCO ICH listing: Jeju Chilmeoridang Yeongdeunggut (ich.unesco.org/en/RL/00187)
Samguk Yusa (Ilyeon, 13th c.) — Dangun cycle
Wikipedia as index (Korean shamanism; Korean creation narratives; Cheonjiwang Bonpuli), claims verified one level down via search
Academic secondary: Seo Daeseok, Myths of Korea; Hyun-key Kim Hogarth, Korean Shamanism and Cultural Nationalism

traditions.name: Native North American traditions (multi-nation umbrella)
traditions.region: North America — Arctic to Southwest; distinct nations incl. Inuit, Tlingit/Haida, Kwakwaka'wakw, Coast Salish, Ojibwe/Cree (Anishinaabe/Algonquian), Haudenosaunee, Wabanaki, Lakota, Cheyenne, Cherokee, Navajo (Diné), Hopi, Zuni
traditions.sensitivity_notes: This is NOT one tradition — dozens of distinct nations with distinct religions, many actively practiced today; every candidate names its source nation in its one_line and must never be genericized as 'Native American'. Living-practice figures (Navajo Changing Woman/Hero Twins, Lakota Wakan Tanka/White Buffalo Calf Woman, Hopi Kokopelli, Anishinaabe manitous) are owner-gated. Specific restriction regimes: Hopi Tribal Council Resolution H-72-94 restricts esoteric katsina/ceremonial knowledge; Navajo cultural norms discourage discussion of skinwalkers and seasonal telling of Coyote stories; Tlingit/Haida/Kwakwaka'wakw narratives and crest beings (Sisiutl, Dzunukwa) are clan-owned property (at.oow), not free folklore; the wendigo carries an active appropriation debate — the antlered deer-skull horror-movie design is a modern non-Native invention, and Algonquian communities treat the concept as a serious moral/spiritual matter, not a stock monster. Source quality 3: rich public-domain ethnography exists (BAE reports: Mooney, Matthews, Swanton, Boas; Rasmussen for Inuit) but much of the deepest material is orally held or formally closed, which caps later-wave extraction. Web verification succeeded for sensitivity claims (wendigo debate, Hopi restrictions); entity list rests on the PD ethnographic corpus with Wikipedia as index.
sources: James Mooney, Myths of the Cherokee (BAE 19th Annual Report, 1900, public domain)
Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897, public domain) — incl. the Great Gambler cycle
John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (BAE Bulletin 39, 1909, public domain)
Franz Boas, Tsimshian Mythology / Kwakiutl ethnography (BAE, public domain)
Knud Rasmussen, Fifth Thule Expedition reports (Inuit intellectual culture)
Hopi Cultural Preservation Office / Hopi Tribal Council Resolution H-72-94 (https://www.hopi-nsn.gov/tribal-services/department-natural-resources-2/hopi-cultural-preservation-office/)
Facing History, 'More Than Monsters: The Deeper Significance of Wendigo Stories' (https://www.facinghistory.org/ideas-week/more-monsters-deeper-significance-wendigo-stories)
Ball State Digital Literature Review, 'The Appropriation of the Windigo Spirit in Horror Literature' (https://openjournals.bsu.edu/dlr/article/download/DLR.7.0.101-112/1771)
Wikipedia (used as index only; claims verified one level down)

traditions.name: Slavic mythology and folklore
traditions.region: Eastern Europe (Russia, Ukraine, Belarus, Poland, Czechia, Slovakia, Balkans)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: No native pre-Christian texts survive — Christianization erased them; the divine layer rests on hostile Christian chroniclers (Primary Chronicle, Thietmar, Helmold, Saxo) plus archaeology, so several 'gods' (Belobog, Lada) have contested historicity — flagged honestly rather than padded. The folklore layer (Baba Yaga, Koschei, house/nature spirits) is by contrast rich, public-domain, and uncontested. Rodnovery (Slavic Native Faith) is an organized revival with registered communities in Russia, Ukraine, Poland and elsewhere — the chief pantheon gods carry the 'living' flag and are owner-gated for on-screen roles; note that some Rodnover groups have nationalist-political associations, so depiction framing deserves care. The 'Book of Veles' cited by some revivalists is a proven modern forgery and was NOT used as a source. Web fetches were limited; ratings rest on search-verified revival status plus my own verified knowledge of the chronicle and folklore corpus, per instructions.
sources: Primary Chronicle / Povest' vremennykh let (Laurentian text, PD translations) — Vladimir's 980 Kyiv pantheon
Thietmar of Merseburg, Chronicon (early 11th c.) — West Slavic cult sites (Radegast/Riedegost, Triglav region)
Helmold of Bosau, Chronica Slavorum (12th c.) — Svetovit, Chernobog passage
Saxo Grammaticus, Gesta Danorum bk. XIV — Svantovit temple at Arkona, horse divination
Procopius, De Bello Gothico VII.14 — earliest notice of Slavic thunder-god worship
Alexander Afanasyev, Narodnye russkie skazki (Russian Fairy Tales, PD) — Baba Yaga, Koschei, Firebird, Vasilisa corpus
Aleksander Gieysztor, Mitologia Słowian (academic secondary)
en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slavic_Native_Faith (index; revival status verified via 2025-era search results)

traditions.name: Haitian Vodou and related traditions
traditions.region: Haiti and the Haitian diaspora (Caribbean, North America, France); related: Louisiana Voodoo, Dominican Vudú
traditions.sensitivity_notes: LIVING minority religion, historically maligned and actively practiced by millions (commonly estimated at a majority of Haiti's ~11M plus diaspora; officially recognized by Haiti in 2003 — 1-100M band = influence ceiling 4). The pop-culture 'voodoo' image (pins-in-dolls, evil curses, black-magic human sacrifice, mindless zombie hordes, cackling witch doctors) is a centuries-old racist horror-movie distortion rooted in post-revolution slander and 1930s Hollywood — the game must NOT reproduce it. Theology matters: Vodou self-describes as monotheistic; the lwa are served spirits under one distant creator (Bondye), NOT a pantheon of 'gods' — the entity_class 'god' below is a game-mechanical label for patron-capable beings and should be softened in-fiction (e.g., lwa attend the Casino as a distinct delegation, not as 'gods'). EVERY lwa carries flag 'living' and depiction_risk living: on-screen roles are owner-gated; default off-screen. It is an initiatory (closed) religion — public-sphere scholarship on the major lwa is legitimate material, but initiation-level detail is not collected (see closed_material_notes); no ritual procedure, vèvè, or song text should ever be depicted as-if-accurate. Gede spirits' genuine association with lottery (bòlèt) number-giving is the honest basis for luck_gambling flags — do not extend gambling theming to other lwa. Source quality 3: rich, solid academic secondary literature (Deren, Métraux, Brown, Hebblethwaite) but primary material is oral/initiatory and partly closed — this honestly caps later-wave extraction depth. Web verification for this census used search results plus indexed reference pages; claims below are limited to well-attested public scholarship, nothing invented.
sources: Maya Deren, Divine Horsemen: The Living Gods of Haiti (1953)
Alfred Métraux, Voodoo in Haiti (1959)
Karen McCarthy Brown, Mama Lola: A Vodou Priestess in Brooklyn (1991)
Benjamin Hebblethwaite, Vodou Songs in Haitian Creole and English (2012)
Encyclopaedia Britannica, 'Lwa (Vodou)' — https://www.britannica.com/topic/lwa-Vodou
Wikipedia (index, verified one level down): Haitian Vodou; Lwa; Rada lwa; Petwo lwa — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haitian_Vodou
The Haitian Times, 'Vodou endures: more than a religion' (2025) — https://haitiantimes.com/2025/08/20/vodou-endures-more-than-a-religion/
Christian Science Monitor, Vodou revival reporting (2024) — https://www.csmonitor.com/Arts-Culture/2024/0510/Voodoo-revival-Haiti-culture-gang-violence

traditions.name: Zoroastrian tradition
traditions.region: Greater Iran (historical); today Mumbai/Gujarat (Parsis), Yazd/Kerman/Tehran (Iranian Zoroastrians), North American diaspora
traditions.sensitivity_notes: LIVING minority faith (~110,000-125,000 adherents; Parsis in India ~50-60k, Iran ~15-25k, N. America ~30k). Influence ceiling honestly 3, not 4: continuous organized worship for ~3,000 years, but total adherents fall well below the rubric's 1M floor for tier 4 (contrast Amaterasu/Shinto=4). All actively-venerated yazatas carry the living flag; on-screen roles for Ahura Mazda and named yazatas should be owner-gated per the living gate. Angra Mainyu/Ahriman is NOT worshipped (worship of the adversary is anathema), so he takes no living flag, but he is the antagonist of a living theology — depicting him as a playable/sympathetic god at the casino tables deserves the same owner review. The prophet Zarathushtra is D-4 excluded (historical human). Source quality 4: full PD English translations of the Avesta and Pahlavi books exist (Darmesteter/Mills SBE 4, 23, 31; West SBE 5, 18, 24, 37, 47) but are dated (1880s-1890s) and the Gathas are notoriously hard; Encyclopaedia Iranica provides strong modern secondary coverage. Web verification was done via search results; principal claims cross-checked against sacred-texts.com indexes and Wikipedia population data.
sources: James Darmesteter (trans.), The Zend-Avesta Parts I-II, Sacred Books of the East vols. 4 & 23 (1880-1883, public domain; sacred-texts.com/zor)
L. H. Mills (trans.), The Zend-Avesta Part III (Yasna, Gathas), SBE vol. 31 (1887, public domain)
E. W. West (trans.), Pahlavi Texts incl. Bundahishn, SBE vols. 5/18/24/37/47 (public domain)
Encyclopaedia Iranica (iranicaonline.org) — academic reference for individual yazatas/daevas
Mary Boyce, Zoroastrians: Their Religious Beliefs and Practices (academic secondary)
Wikipedia (index only; claims verified one level down): Zoroastrianism, List of countries by Zoroastrian population
worldpopulationreview.com Zoroastrian population by country (demographics cross-check)

traditions.name: Aboriginal Australian traditions (the Dreaming / Tjukurpa)
traditions.region: Australia — continent-wide; hundreds of distinct nations (Gamilaraay, Wiradjuri, Kulin, Noongar, Yolngu, Arrernte, Kimberley peoples, etc.), not one pantheon
traditions.sensitivity_notes: This is a LIVING tradition with active ceremonial law, so every ancestral being listed carries depiction_risk of living-or-restricted; on-screen use is owner-gated at minimum. Large parts of the corpus are secret-sacred (gender-restricted, initiation-restricted, or clan-owned) and are deliberately NOT collected here — see closed_material_notes. The published record is uneven: much of it is colonial-era ethnography (Langloh Parker, Ramsay Smith, Warner, the Berndts) collected under questionable consent and now subject to AIATSIS access/repatriation protocols; modern general-audience retellings are shallow. Source_quality 2 reflects that honestly — later waves should extract very little from this tradition. 'Rainbow Serpent' etc. are pan-Australian umbrella labels covering many distinct clan-owned beings (Wagyl, Yurlungur, Ngalyod...); flattening them into one 'god' is itself a simplification the owner should be aware of. Research note: Wikipedia fetches returned 403 through the proxy; verification relied on search-result content (Wikipedia index, WIPO, Creative Spirits, EBSCO) plus my own knowledge cross-checked against those results. No luck/fortune/gambling deity and no wagering-or-unmaking myth exists in the published corpus, so no luck_gambling or forsaken_candidate flags are assigned — that absence is genuine, not an oversight.
sources: Wikipedia: List of Australian Aboriginal mythological figures (used as INDEX only; claims cross-checked)
Wikipedia: Rainbow Serpent; Daramulan
WIPO Magazine — 'Safeguarding Cultural Heritage: The Case of the Sacred Wandjina' (documents depiction restrictions)
Creative Spirits (creativespirits.info) — Wandjina and cultural-protocol pages
EBSCO Research Starters — Rainbow Serpent (deity)
K. Langloh Parker, 'Australian Legendary Tales' (1896, public domain; colonial-era caveats apply)
W. Ramsay Smith, 'Myths and Legends of the Australian Aboriginals' (1930, PD; colonial-era caveats)
R.M. & C.H. Berndt, 'The World of the First Australians' (academic reference)
A.W. Reed, 'Aboriginal Myths, Legends and Fables' (general-audience reference)
AIATSIS cultural-sensitivity and access protocols (framework for what is closed)

traditions.name: Cosmic Horror Corpus (Lovecraft Circle / Public-Domain Mythos)
traditions.region: United States / transatlantic weird fiction, 1895–1971 (Bierce/Chambers through Lovecraft circle and Derleth)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: No living worship — chaos-magick and satirical 'Cthulhu cults' exist but are ironic/occasional, not a religion; influence honestly floors at 1 across the family. SHIP STATUS: whole family is researched-now-deferred per owner ruling — every candidate carries an ip flag. IP boundary verified via web search 2026-07-18: all Lovecraft works published before 1930 (incl. The Call of Cthulhu, 1928) are US public domain as of 2026-01-01; Lovecraft-solo works 1930–1937 (Innsmouth, Mountains of Madness) show no copyright-renewal records and are widely treated as PD, but were published post-1928 — I flagged them ip:public_domain_literary with the year noted in one_line for owner review. Bierce (1893) and Chambers (1895) material (Hastur, King in Yellow, Yellow Sign) is unambiguously PD. Derleth-era and other-hand additions (Cthugha, Ithaqua, Byakhee — Derleth estate/Arkham House; Tsathoggua — Clark Ashton Smith estate/CASiana) are ip:modern_ip_flagged with claimant in one_line. Living-author mythos (Ramsey Campbell, etc.) excluded entirely — see closed notes. Source quality 5: complete PD primary texts freely available (hplovecraft.com, Project Gutenberg) plus strong scholarship (S.T. Joshi).
sources: H.P. Lovecraft collected fiction, PD primary texts (hplovecraft.com/writings/texts/ and Project Gutenberg)
Robert W. Chambers, The King in Yellow (1895, PD); Ambrose Bierce, 'An Inhabitant of Carcosa'/'Haita the Shepherd' (1890s, PD)
S.T. Joshi, 'I Am Providence' and 'The Rise, Fall, and Rise of the Cthulhu Mythos' (academic secondary)
Copyright status: legalclarity.org/are-h-p-lovecrafts-works-in-the-public-domain/ and chaosium.com/fan-use-and-licensing-q-a/ (verified via WebSearch 2026-07-18)
lovecraft.fandom.com used only as an index, claims verified against primary texts

traditions.name: Internet-era folklore (creepypasta, SCP, Slenderman-class, analog horror)
traditions.region: Global (anglophone internet origin; 4chan / Something Awful / Wikidot / YouTube ecosystems, 2004–present)
traditions.sensitivity_notes: SHIP STATUS: researched-now-deferred (owner ruling) — nothing here ships without separate IP clearance. This is not a religious-sensitivity tradition (no living worship, depiction_risk none across the board); its sensitivity is (a) LEGAL and (b) real-world-harm adjacency. IP map key: ip:cc_licensed = SCP Wiki material, CC BY-SA 3.0 — free commercial use IS permitted but the game's derivative SCP content must itself be CC BY-SA 3.0 and attributed (share-alike is viral; incompatible with a closed commercial asset pipeline unless SCP content is cleanly quarantined). CRITICAL SCP carve-out: SCP-173's famous image is Izumi Kato's sculpture 'Untitled 2004', NEVER licensed — the wiki decoupled it in 2022; text only. ip:modern_ip_flagged = active, identifiable claimant: Slender Man (Eric Knudsen created/registered 2009-10; Sony Screen Gems bought film rights; scope of the character copyright is legally contested but Sony has sent C&Ds — treat as claimed); Siren Head / Cartoon Cat (Trevor Henderson, actively enforced, explicitly refused CC/SCP absorption); Skibidi Toilet (rights assigned by creator Alexey Gerasimov to Invisible Narratives/Michael Bay 2023; Next Level Apps trademark grab defeated by settlement June 2026; creator-control dispute ongoing July 2026 — most legally radioactive entry on this list); Momo (image is the 'Mother Bird' sculpture by Keisuke Aisawa / Link Factory, Japan — sculpture destroyed 2019, copyright persists); Ben Drowned (creator Alex Hall known AND entangled with Nintendo's Majora's Mask); Sonic.exe (known author + Sega character entanglement); Candle Cove/Skin-Taker (Kris Straub, licensed to Syfy's Channel Zero); Zalgo (Dave Kelly, 2004); Loab (Steph Maj Swanson); This Man (Andrea Natella marketing hoax); Herobrine (character disclaimed by Mojang but inseparable from Minecraft trade dress — Microsoft adjacency). ip:public_domain_literary is used here in a NON-STANDARD sense the owner must understand: anonymous collective 4chan/forum works (The Rake, Jeff the Killer text, Smile.jpg, Russian Sleep Experiment, Backrooms core concept, RNGesus) are ORPHAN/collective works with no known enforceable claimant, not formally public domain — lowest risk in the family but not zero. The Backrooms specifically: core concept/imagery anonymous; A24 publicly disclaimed ownership of the yellow-wallpaper concept and community works (July 2026, after DMCA backlash), but the Kane Parsons/A24 film franchise is owned, and Backrooms wiki entities are CC BY-SA 3.0. Real-world-harm adjacency: Slender Man is tied to the 2014 Waukesha stabbing (real minors, real victim — never reference); Momo to a child-harm hoax panic; Jeff the Killer's image to an unverified rumor about a real deceased person. Research method note: web searches succeeded through the proxy; IP claims above verified against July 2026 news; entity descriptions rest on well-documented, stable wiki/press primaries.
sources: SCP Wiki licensing guide (scp-wiki.wikidot.com/licensing-guide — CC BY-SA 3.0; SCP-173 image carve-out)
Wikipedia: Slender Man, Herobrine, Trevor Henderson, Skibidi Toilet, The Backrooms, Creepypasta (used as index, claims verified one level down)
TheWrap / Techdirt / Hollywood Reporter — Sony Slender Man C&D litigation (2018)
TheWrap 'A24 Backs Off Backrooms Copyright Claim After Kane Parsons Intervenes' + Creative Bloq + PC Gamer (July 2026)
Bloody Disgusting 'Siren Head Explained' + Trevor Henderson public statements refusing SCP/CC licensing
Yahoo Finance / Las Vegas Sun — Invisible Narratives v. Next Level Apps Skibidi Toilet settlement (June 2026); Popverse / TheGamer / Dexerto — Gerasimov creator-control dispute (2026)
Minecraft Wiki + Minecraft Forum — Herobrine origin and Mojang non-claim
